mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-Benz GLC 250 or 300 petrol engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned M274 engine typically costs £3,000–£5,500 supply only; a reconditioned M264 costs £3,500–£7,500. Used units are available from £1,800–£3,500. Reconditioned M264 engines start from around £3,495. Total installed cost for a reconditioned engine is typically around £1,800–£2,800. Fitting adds £1,500–£2,500 depending on the workshop. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your GLC.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-Benz GLC 250 / 300 petrol engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M274 and M264 are generally reliable engines but have known weaknesses. The M274 suffers from thermostat housing failure (the most common issue), PCV system failure (expensive to replace), timing chain stretch on neglected oil intervals, and camshaft actuator wear. The M264 is more modern with EQ Boost but has reports of camshaft centre valve filter issues. With diligent maintenance, both can be reliable.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz GLC 250 and 300 petrol?

acceptedAnswer.text: M274: thermostat housing failure (coolant loss, P0128/P0597), PCV system failure (rough idle, whistling, P052E), timing chain stretch (rattling on cold start), camshaft actuator wear, turbocharger failure at higher mileages. M264: camshaft centre valve filter issues, cooling system concerns. The M274's PCV system and timing chain are the most critical concerns.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-Benz GLC 250 / 300 petrol engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: GLC values remain strong — a well-kept X253 is often worth £12,000–£25,000+. A £3,000–£7,500 reconditioned engine is 15–40% of the vehicle's value — apply the Mercedes 70% scrap threshold. If the car is a cherished example with good bodywork, replacement is often worthwhile.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz GLC 250 or 300 petrol engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. X253 pre-facelift (2015–2019) uses M274; X253 facelift (2019–2022) uses M264. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M274 timing chain, or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The timing chain and tensioner can be replaced — they are wear items. Replacement costs £1,200–£2,500. The M274 uses a dual-row chain, which is more robust than the single-row M271, but it can still stretch, especially on neglected oil change intervals. If the chain fails and jumps timing, the engine is typically destroyed. A preventative chain replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement (£3,000+). At the first sign of rattling on cold start, book an inspection immediately.

Both are smooth and refined, but thermostat housing failures, timing chain stretch and PCV system issues are well-documented. replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz GLC 250 / 300 Petrol Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the current GLC 250/300 petrol market. Reconditioned M264 engines start from around £3,495. M274 engines typically sit at the lower end of the range; M264 engines command a premium. Total installed cost for a reconditioned engine is typically around £1,800–£2,800.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a GLC 250/300 petrol engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The engine bay is relatively accessible compared to V6 and V8 variants.

cta.label: → Compare GLC 250 / 300 petrol engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Thermostat Housing Failure (M274 & M264)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss with no visible external leak, rising temperature gauge, engine warning light (P0128, P0597), heater not working properly
problems.recommendation: The thermostat housing is the most common problem on the M274 engine. It is prone to failure and coolant loss, which can lead to overheating if not addressed. The thermostat is electric and located under the engine, making replacement more labour-intensive than on older engines. If you notice coolant loss or a warning light, have the thermostat housing inspected immediately. Catching it early prevents more serious overheating damage.

failureLink.label: Read the full thermostat failure guide

problems.title: PCV System Failure (M274)
problems.symptoms: Rough idle, engine warning light, whistling noise from the engine, oil consumption, P052E code
problems.recommendation: The M274 engine is "burdened with an expensive replacement of the PCV system failure". Repair costs can be significant — some owners report estimates exceeding $5,000 USD. The PCV system is integrated into the cylinder head, making replacement labour-intensive. If you experience rough idle, a whistling noise, or a P052E code, have the PCV system inspected promptly. Early intervention can prevent more serious oil consumption issues.

failureLink.label: Read the full PCV failure guide

problems.title: Timing Chain Stretch (M274)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start, metallic ticking from the front of the engine, engine warning light, cam timing errors
problems.replacementCost: £3,000–£5,500 (if chain jumps and causes valve interference)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if the timing chain fails, the engine is typically destroyed

problems.recommendation: The M274's timing chain lifespan is approximately 80k miles (plus or minus 20k). Timing chain stretch is a known issue, particularly on neglected oil change intervals. The M274 uses a dual-row chain, which is more robust than the single-row M271, but it is not immune to wear. If you hear rattling on cold start, book an inspection immediately. A preventative chain replacement is far cheaper than a full engine replacement.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: GLC 250 / 300 Petrol — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Expensive but cheaper than a new engine
Labour-intensive but far cheaper than a full engine replacement
If chain jumps and valves hit pistons, replacement is the only option

buyingChecks.title: Buying This GLC 250 / 300 Petrol? Check These First
buyIf: * Facelift X253 (2019+) with M264 engine — more modern with EQ Boost and improved efficiency
* M274 car with evidence of thermostat housing and PCV system work — the common weaknesses addressed
* Full service history with regular oil changes (every 10,000 miles) — timing chain health depends on it
* Evidence of timing chain inspection or replacement — especially important on higher-mileage M274 cars
avoidIf: * M274 with no proof of PCV system work — expensive replacement may be imminent
* Any rattling on cold start — timing chain stretch is likely
* M274 with coolant loss and no diagnosis — thermostat housing failure is common
* Poor service history — the M274 and M264 are sensitive to oil quality

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The GLC 250 and 300 petrols are powered by two generations of Mercedes 2.0-litre turbocharged four-cylinders. The M274 (2015–2019) is generally reliable but has known weaknesses: thermostat housing failure (the most common issue), PCV system failure (expensive to replace), timing chain stretch on neglected oil intervals, and camshaft actuator wear. The M264 (2019–2022) replaced the M274 with EQ Boost technology, offering improved efficiency but with reports of camshaft centre valve filter issues. Buy on history, maintain on schedule, and these engines will reward you with smooth, refined performance. Skip the history and it will punish you.

cta.label: → Compare GLC 250 / 300 petrol engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz GLC 250 or 300 petrol (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: 1. Cold Start — Listen for rattles from the front of the engine (timing chain warning on M274)
2. Service History — including which generation (M274 or M264) and evidence of major maintenance
3. Thermostat/Coolant — Any coolant loss or thermostat warning lights? (the most common M274 issue)
4. PCV System (M274) — Any rough idle or whistling? (PCV failure is expensive to repair)
5. Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues (P0128, P0597, P052E)?
6. Oil Level — Any excessive oil consumption? (PCV or piston ring issues)
7. Camshaft Actuators (M274) — Any noise on cold start? (wear is common after 60–70k miles)
8. MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (catalyst, O2 sensors)
